title: Sales and profit show a clear upward level shift from 2020 to 2021
description: The dataset shows a broad year-over-year step-change rather than a mild increase: monthly Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold are higher in every 2021 month than in the corresponding 2020 month. This indicates that growth was sustained across the full year, not concentrated in just one season. Step 4 explicitly identifies this as a level shift, and Step 3 supports it by showing that Total Sales and Operating Profit move closely together (correlation 0.956) while Units Sold is also strongly tied to sales (correlation 0.845).

data_evidence.key_statistics: Monthly sums for Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold are higher in every month of 2021 than the matching month of 2020; Step 3 correlations: Units Sold-Total Sales = 0.844936119178483, Total Sales-Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: 'Broad step-change upward from 2020 to 2021 in transaction volume and totals.'

title: 2020 weakened into mid-year before recovering in late summer
description: In 2020, business activity declined from early-year levels into June, then rebounded from July through September before softening again later in the year. This trend appears across Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold, suggesting a broad operational slowdown followed by a partial recovery rather than a product- or margin-specific issue. Step 4 provides the temporal pattern, while Step 3 indicates that volume is a major driver because Units Sold is strongly correlated with both Total Sales (0.845) and Operating Profit (0.814).

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 correlations: Units Sold-Total Sales = 0.844936119178483; Units Sold-Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: '2020 mid-year trough followed by partial rebound' with declines into June and recovery in July-September.

title: 2021 sales and profit bottomed in March and peaked around July-August
description: In 2021, Total Sales and Operating Profit declined from January to March, then climbed through spring and early summer, reaching their highest levels around July and August before easing in autumn. This creates a clear U-shaped first-half pattern followed by a late-year moderation. The tight relationship between sales and profit in Step 3 (correlation 0.956) reinforces that the profit trend largely followed the revenue trend.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales and Operating Profit are strongly correlated at 0.9563074748281619 in Step 3.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: '2021 sales and profit trough in March, then climb to summer peak.'

title: Units Sold consistently reaches its annual high in August
description: The highest monthly Units Sold total occurs in August in both 2020 and 2021, indicating a repeated late-summer demand peak rather than a one-off spike. Because Units Sold is strongly linked to Total Sales (correlation 0.845) and Operating Profit (0.814), this recurring August volume high is likely a major contributor to the broader summer strength seen in financial metrics.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 correlations: Units Sold-Total Sales = 0.844936119178483; Units Sold-Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: 'Units sold peak in late summer in both years' and specifically 'the highest monthly Units Sold sums occur in August in both 2020 and 2021.'

title: After the 2021 summer peak, performance softened in September-October before recovering late in the year
description: Multiple metrics weakened after summer highs in 2021, with Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold declining through September and October, then stabilizing or recovering afterward. Step 4 further notes that the rebound becomes visible in November and is especially strong in December. This suggests an autumn slowdown followed by a year-end recovery cycle.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies both an autumn slowdown and a late-year recovery; Step 3 shows Total Sales and Operating Profit remain tightly linked at 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Patterns identified in Step 4: 'Repeated autumn softening in 2021 after summer highs' and 'Late-year recovery in 2021 after autumn slowdown,' with recovery in November and especially December.

title: Average price in 2021 was weakest in Q1 and climbed steadily to a December high
description: Price per Unit followed a rising pattern through most of 2021: average monthly pricing was lowest in the first quarter and generally increased through the rest of the year, ending at the highest point in December. This indicates that late-year revenue strength was supported not only by volume recovery but also by stronger pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies the lowest average monthly Price per Unit in early 2021 and the yearly high in December.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: 'Price per Unit mean declines early in 2021, then trends upward through year-end.'

title: 2020 pricing rose into June, then dropped sharply and stayed below the mid-year peak
description: Average Price per Unit increased from January to a June high in 2020, then fell sharply by August and remained below the June level for the rest of the year. This suggests a distinct pricing regime change in the second half of 2020 rather than normal month-to-month fluctuation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies a June high followed by a sharp August drop and lower pricing through year-end.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: '2020 price spike into June followed by lower second-half pricing.'

title: 2021 generated higher monthly totals even though average sales and profit per record were lower than 2020 peaks
description: Monthly Total Sales and Operating Profit sums were much larger in 2021, but the mean Total Sales and mean Operating Profit per record were generally lower than 2020 peaks and were lower year-over-year in comparable early months. This implies that 2021 growth came more from a higher number of transactions than from larger individual transactions. Step 4 highlights the contrast between totals and per-record averages, and Step 3 notes that distributions are concentrated in a small number of very large transactions, making this distinction especially important.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that mean-based summaries overstate typical transaction size because revenue and profit are concentrated in a relatively small number of very large transactions.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: 'Per-record sales and profit are generally lower in 2021 than in 2020 despite higher monthly totals.'

title: Retailer mix stayed stable over time even as transaction activity changed sharply
description: The monthly mean of Retailer ID stayed in a narrow band across the full period, while transaction counts changed dramatically between 2020 and 2021. This suggests that the overall retailer composition remained relatively stable and that the major trend was growth in activity volume rather than a major shift in which retailers dominated the dataset.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 notes that Retailer ID mean is very stable over time while count changes sharply.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern identified in Step 4: 'Retailer ID mean is very stable over time while count changes sharply.'

title: Operating Profit shows the most concentrated anomaly burden with 706 extreme records
description: Step 2 flagged Operating Profit as having 706 outliers, representing 7.32% of records, making it the single largest outlier issue among the core financial measures. This is anomalous because profit is a primary business KPI, and a 7.32% extreme-value rate is high enough to materially distort average profitability, retailer comparisons, and exception reporting.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 706 outliers in Operating Profit; 7.32% of records; severity rated high.
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit outlier count = 706, higher than Total Sales (653), Units Sold (627), Price per Unit (81), and Operating Margin (44).

title: Total Sales has 653 anomalous high-value records, indicating revenue spikes are not isolated
description: According to Step 2, Total Sales contains 653 outliers, or 6.77% of all records. This is a significant anomaly rate for a revenue field and suggests that unusually large transactions are common enough to skew mean sales metrics and create misleading impressions of typical transaction size.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 653 outliers in Total Sales; 6.77% of records; severity rated high.
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales outlier count = 653, indicating roughly 1 in 15 records is statistically extreme.

title: Units Sold has 627 outliers, pointing to repeated extreme-volume transactions
description: Step 2 identified 627 outliers in Units Sold, equal to 6.50% of records. This indicates that extreme order sizes are not rare events but a recurring anomaly pattern, which can distort demand analysis, inventory planning, and productivity benchmarks if treated as typical sales behavior.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 627 outliers in Units Sold; 6.50% of records; severity rated high.
data_evidence.data_points: Units Sold outlier count = 627, only slightly below Total Sales (653), consistent with volume-driven transaction extremes.

title: Price per Unit anomalies are rare but still material in a key pricing field

data_evidence.key_statistics: 81 outliers in Price per Unit; 0.84% of records; severity rated medium.
data_evidence.data_points: Price per Unit outlier count = 81, far below Total Sales (653) and Operating Profit (706), suggesting transaction-size anomalies are more prevalent than pricing anomalies.

title: Operating Margin is relatively stable, with only 44 anomalous records

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outliers = 706; Total Sales outliers = 653; difference = 53 records.

This means anomalously large profit records are likely concentrated among anomalously large sales transactions rather than arising independently, reinforcing that a small set of oversized transactions is driving much of the financial extremity.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.

data_evidence.data_points: The much larger outlier count in Units Sold than in Price per Unit suggests extreme revenue records are more often quantity-driven than price-driven.

title: A small number of very large transactions create a global anomaly pattern across revenue and profit

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.

This makes revenue growth a strong proxy for profit growth in this dataset, though Step 2 warns that 706 Operating Profit outliers and 653 Total Sales outliers could amplify some extreme points.

Combined with Step 2's 81 price outliers (0.84%), this makes June 2020 a notable local anomaly period in pricing behavior, even though price anomalies are relatively rare overall.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Average Price per Unit reaches its 2020 high in June, then declines sharply by August and remains below the June level afterward.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 also reports 81 Price per Unit outliers (0.84%), showing the pricing spike occurs within a generally low-anomaly pricing field.

title: Operating Profit tracks Total Sales almost proportionally across transactions
description: The strongest correlation in the dataset is between Total Sales and Operating Profit at 0.956, indicating that higher-revenue transactions almost always translate into higher operating profit as well. This is a tighter relationship than any pairing involving Units Sold, so revenue is the closest direct transactional companion to profit in this data. Given the quality review also found 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers, the relationship appears strong even in the presence of many extreme records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.9563074748281619; Total Sales outliers = 653; Operating Profit outliers = 706.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 lists this as the strongest observed correlation; Step 2 shows both variables have similarly high outlier counts.

title: Units Sold has a stronger link to revenue than to profit
description: Units Sold correlates 0.845 with Total Sales but 0.814 with Operating Profit. The 0.031 gap shows that transaction volume connects more directly to revenue generation than to profit generation, which likely passes through additional pricing or cost effects before becoming profit. This makes Units Sold a better leading indicator for sales than for profitability at the transaction level.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.844936119178483 versus Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.8138708043211902; difference = 0.0310653148572928.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies both relationships as strong, with the sales link clearly higher.

title: Revenue acts as the key bridge between volume and profit
description: The dataset shows a chain of strong positive relationships: Units Sold to Total Sales is 0.845, Units Sold to Operating Profit is 0.814, and Total Sales to Operating Profit is 0.956. Because the sales-profit correlation is much higher than the units-profit correlation by 0.142, profit appears to be connected to volume mainly through revenue rather than through unit count alone.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.8138708043211902; Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.9563074748281619; gap = 0.1424366705069717.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 reports all three structural correlations, allowing comparison of direct and indirect links.

title: Profit shows slightly more extreme-value exposure than sales
description: Operating Profit has 706 outliers, versus 653 for Total Sales, a difference of 53 records. That means profit has about 8.1% more flagged extremes than sales (706 vs 653), suggesting profitability outcomes are somewhat more uneven than revenue outcomes even though the two measures are tightly linked.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%); Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier count difference = 53 records; outlier rate difference = 0.55 percentage points.

title: Volume extremes are nearly as common as revenue extremes
description: Units Sold has 627 outliers compared with 653 for Total Sales, only 26 fewer records. The outlier rates are also close at 6.50% versus 6.77%, indicating that unusually large sales values are accompanied by similarly frequent volume spikes rather than being driven mainly by rare pricing events.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold outliers = 627 (6.50%); Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier count gap = 26 records; outlier rate gap = 0.27 percentage points.

title: Pricing anomalies are rare compared with transaction-scale anomalies
description: Price per Unit has just 81 outliers (0.84%), far below Units Sold at 627 (6.50%) and Total Sales at 653 (6.77%). In count terms, sales has 572 more outliers than price, and units has 546 more. This shows the distribution of unusual transactions is concentrated much more in order size and sales value than in unit pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers = 81 (0.84%); Units Sold = 627 (6.50%); Total Sales = 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Sales-price outlier gap = 572; units-price outlier gap = 546; rate gaps = 5.93 and 5.66 percentage points.

title: Margin anomalies are dramatically less frequent than profit anomalies
description: Operating Margin has only 44 outliers (0.46%) while Operating Profit has 706 (7.32%). That is a 662-record gap, and profit’s outlier rate is nearly 16 times higher than margin’s (7.32% / 0.46% ≈ 15.9). The distribution of profitability dollars is therefore far more uneven than the distribution of profitability rates.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers = 44 (0.46%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier count gap = 662; relative outlier-rate multiple ≈ 15.9x.

title: Sales is the strongest direct benchmark for profit among the core numeric measures
description: The correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit is 0.956, which is materially higher than the correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit at 0.814. The gap of 0.142 indicates profit distribution aligns much more closely with revenue distribution than with raw volume distribution.

data_evidence.key_statistics: corr(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.956; corr(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.814.
data_evidence.data_points: Correlation gap = 0.142.

title: Revenue tracks volume more closely than profit does
description: Units Sold correlates with Total Sales at 0.845, compared with 0.814 for Units Sold versus Operating Profit. While both are strong relationships, the 0.031 higher correlation with sales shows revenue distribution is slightly more directly shaped by volume than profit distribution is.

data_evidence.key_statistics: corr(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.845; corr(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.814.
data_evidence.data_points: Correlation difference = 0.031.

title: Profit is almost one-for-one aligned with sales relative to other major pairings
description: Among the reported strong relationships, Total Sales and Operating Profit have the highest correlation at 0.956, exceeding Units Sold with Total Sales at 0.845 and Units Sold with Operating Profit at 0.814. This makes the sales-profit pairing stronger by 0.111 and 0.142 respectively, indicating the closest distributional alignment in the dataset is between revenue and profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Top correlations: Sales-Profit = 0.956; Units-Sales = 0.845; Units-Profit = 0.814.
data_evidence.data_points: Sales-Profit exceeds Units-Sales by 0.111 and Units-Profit by 0.142.

title: The anomaly burden is concentrated in three high-impact amount and volume fields
description: Operating Profit, Total Sales, and Units Sold account for 706, 653, and 627 outliers respectively, all above 6.5% of records. By contrast, Price per Unit and Operating Margin are much lower at 81 and 44 outliers. The spread from the highest to lowest anomaly count is 662 records, showing a sharp distributional split between scale-based measures and rate-based measures.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outlier counts: Operating Profit 706, Total Sales 653, Units Sold 627, Price per Unit 81, Operating Margin 44.
data_evidence.data_points: Highest-lowest gap = 662 records; top three all exceed 6.5% while bottom two are below 1%.

title: Profit tracks sales almost one-for-one across transactions
description: A clear structural pattern in the full dataset is that Operating Profit rises almost proportionally with Total Sales. Step 3 reports a correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, which is substantially stronger than the 0.845 correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales and the 0.814 correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit. This indicates that once revenue is generated, profit tends to move in a very consistent direction with it across records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.956; Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.845; Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.814.
data_evidence.data_points: The sales-profit relationship is the strongest measured pairwise relationship in the dataset, exceeding the next strongest correlation by 0.111.

title: Volume influences revenue more strongly than it influences profit directly
description: The dataset shows a consistent pattern where Units Sold is more tightly linked to Total Sales than to Operating Profit. Step 3 shows a 0.845 correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales versus 0.814 between Units Sold and Operating Profit, a gap of 0.031. This suggests volume primarily converts into revenue first, with profit then following through the revenue relationship rather than volume alone.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.845; Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.814.
data_evidence.data_points: Units-to-sales correlation exceeds units-to-profit correlation by 0.031.

title: Profit shows slightly more extreme behavior than sales despite their tight linkage
description: A notable pattern is that Operating Profit and Total Sales move very closely together, yet profit still exhibits slightly more anomaly exposure. Step 2 identifies 706 outliers in Operating Profit (7.32%) versus 653 outliers in Total Sales (6.77%), a difference of 53 records and 0.55 percentage points. This indicates that profitability outcomes are generally aligned with revenue, but profit has an added layer of volatility at the extremes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%); Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit has 53 more outliers than Total Sales, with anomaly incidence higher by 0.55 percentage points.

title: Extreme transaction patterns are concentrated in scale metrics rather than price
description: The anomaly profile shows a strong pattern: transaction scale fields are far more prone to extremes than pricing. Step 2 reports 627 outliers in Units Sold (6.50%) and 653 in Total Sales (6.77%), while Price per Unit has only 81 outliers (0.84%). That means sales anomalies occur about 8.1 times as often as price anomalies, and units anomalies occur about 7.8 times as often. This points to unusually large orders or aggregated transactions as a recurring pattern, rather than widespread pricing irregularity.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold outliers = 627 (6.50%); Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%); Price per Unit outliers = 81 (0.84%).
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales anomaly rate is about 8.1x Price per Unit; Units Sold anomaly rate is about 7.8x Price per Unit.

title: Revenue and profit both follow a heavy-tail pattern, with profit the more extreme tail
description: The dataset exhibits a repeated heavy-tail pattern in financial outcomes: most records are much smaller, while a minority of transactions are exceptionally large. Step 2 shows 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%). Combined with Step 3's note that mean-based summaries materially overstate typical transaction size and profitability, this indicates both financial measures are heavily right-skewed, with Operating Profit having the slightly heavier extreme tail.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit exceeds Total Sales by 53 outlier records, reinforcing a slightly heavier extreme tail in profit.

title: Price anomalies are uncommon relative to other core business measures
description: A clear dataset-wide pattern is that Price per Unit is much less likely to display extreme values than volume, revenue, or profit. Step 2 finds only 81 price outliers (0.84%), compared with 627 in Units Sold (6.50%), 653 in Total Sales (6.77%), and 706 in Operating Profit (7.32%). This means the anomaly rate for profit is roughly 8.7 times the anomaly rate for price, indicating pricing is comparatively controlled while transaction scale varies much more widely.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers = 81 (0.84%); Units Sold = 627 (6.50%); Total Sales = 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit anomaly rate is about 8.7x the Price per Unit anomaly rate.
